skill.name: schedule-with-google-calendar
skill.description: Use Rarefriend MCP to schedule Google Calendar meetings, find free time, manage events, resolve attendees from contacts, and log meeting notes.
skill.summary: Schedule Google Calendar events through Rarefriend MCP with contact-aware availability checks.
skill.author: Whitefield-Labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are Markdown examples, MCP tool names, product URLs, and client setup paths rather than malicious code. The remaining risk is medium because the skill directs users to run a community MCP package and handle Google Calendar credentials.

medium_findings.title: Community MCP Package Executes Through npx
medium_findings.description: The setup instructions run @rarefriend-ai/mcp through npx. This is expected for the integration, but it executes a community npm package on the user machine and creates supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx command is explicit and semantically required by setup. It appears legitimate, but external package execution is a real residual risk for a community skill.

medium_findings.title: Calendar Credentials Stored in Local MCP Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill requires a Rarefriend client ID and client secret and shows users how to place them in local MCP configuration files. This is necessary setup, but mishandled config files could expose calendar access credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly document secret environment variables and local client configuration paths. This is not malicious, but the sensitivity of OAuth credentials makes the risk concrete.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Calendar and Contact Data Access
medium_findings.description: The workflows use Rarefriend MCP to access Google Calendar, contacts, attendee emails, and meeting notes. This matches the skill purpose, but it involves sensitive personal and scheduling data handled by a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflows explicitly combine calendar operations, contact lookup, and note creation. The intent is legitimate, but the data types are sensitive.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service URLs Are Expected Product Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to rarefriend.com and the public source repository. I found no evidence that these URLs receive hidden telemetry or credential exfiltration in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible setup and documentation links. No code or instruction sends secrets to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography patterns, but the reviewed lines contain metadata, Markdown text, client config paths, or secret variable names. No cryptographic algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API calls. The static matches are context-free Markdown false positives.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines describe integration checks, npm setup, and calendar availability checks. They do not collect host details or enumerate the user system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is MCP connection detection and calendar free-time lookup. I found no evidence of host reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Package Execution in Setup Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup flow uses npx with @rarefriend-ai/mcp. Users should verify the package source before running it because npm execution can run package code locally.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is documented directly and is the only confirmed local execution path. The risk depends on trust in the community package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive OAuth Values in Local Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup documentation places RAREFRIEND_CLIENT_ID and RAREFRIEND_CLIENT_SECRET in local MCP configuration. This should be treated as sensitive credential storage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret variable names are explicit and repeated in setup examples. This is expected behavior, not malware, but it is a concrete handling risk.

content.value_statement: Calendar scheduling often requires checking availability, finding attendee emails, and avoiding conflicts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rarefriend MCP workflows for Google Calendar scheduling.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether Rarefriend MCP and Google Calendar are connected.
Finds upcoming Google Calendar events and searches events by keyword or date range.
Finds available meeting times and checks attendee availability when emails are provided.
Creates, reschedules, and cancels Google Calendar events after confirmation.
Resolves attendee names through Rarefriend contacts and can log notes after meetings.
limitations: Requires Rarefriend MCP, Rarefriend credentials, and a connected Google Calendar account.
Cannot schedule correctly if contacts lack current email addresses.
Does not replace user approval for sensitive scheduling changes.
Depends on Rarefriend and Google Calendar service availability.

use_cases.title: Schedule Client Calls
use_cases.description: Find a free slot, resolve the client contact, and create a Google Calendar event with attendees.

use_cases.title: Protect Focus Time
use_cases.description: Find open time blocks and create calendar holds that match the user schedule preferences.

use_cases.title: Prepare Relationship Context
use_cases.description: Search contacts, schedule follow-up meetings, and log notes after calls for future context.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Schedule
prompt_templates.prompt: What is on my Google Calendar today? Summarize the events by time and highlight meetings with attendees.

prompt_templates.title: Book a Simple Meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Schedule a 30 minute call with Alice next week. Find her email, check availability, and ask me to confirm the best slot.

prompt_templates.title: Reschedule an Existing Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Find my meeting with Marcus this week and move it to the next available afternoon slot. Confirm the new time before changing it.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Attendee Availability
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a 60 minute time next week for me, Priya, and Daniel. Resolve their emails, check attendee availability, and propose three options.

output_examples.input: Schedule a 30 minute call with Alice next Tuesday afternoon.
output_examples.output: I found Alice in your contacts and checked Tuesday afternoon. The best open slot is 2:30 PM to 3:00 PM. Please confirm before I create the event.

output_examples.input: What is on my calendar this week?
output_examples.output: You have nine events this week. Monday is meeting-heavy, Wednesday has a free afternoon, and Friday has one external call.

output_examples.input: Log notes after my call with Marcus.
output_examples.output: I found Marcus in your contacts and added the meeting note to his relationship history for future reference.

best_practices: Confirm event details with the user before creating, rescheduling, or canceling calendar events.
Resolve names to email addresses before checking multi-attendee availability.
Treat Rarefriend client secrets and local MCP configuration files as sensitive credentials.
anti_patterns: Do not create events before checking availability when the user asks for a meeting slot.
Do not guess attendee emails when contact search returns no confident match.
Do not expose client secrets, access links, or private calendar details in shared output.

faq.question: Does this skill connect directly to Google Calendar?
faq.answer: It uses Rarefriend MCP, which must be connected to Google Calendar before calendar workflows can run.

faq.question: Can it find attendee emails from names?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs the assistant to search Rarefriend contacts before scheduling with named attendees.

faq.question: Can it prevent double booking?
faq.answer: It is designed to call availability checks before creating meetings, including attendee availability when emails are available.

faq.question: Can it cancel or reschedule events?
faq.answer: Yes. It searches for the event first, then uses Rarefriend MCP calendar tools to reschedule or cancel it.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: Users need Rarefriend MCP client credentials and an authorized Google Calendar connection.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive calendars?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow handles calendar events, contacts, and notes through a third-party MCP service.
